planting.note: Evergreen silver shrub with clusters of yellow everlasting flowers.

directions: Sow onto a well-drained surface mix and barely cover, allowing time for uneven germination. Grow in full sun and gritty soil, watering sparingly once the shrub is established. Trim after flowering to keep the mound of woolly silver leaves compact; dense clusters of golden button flowers appear through summer.

planting.note: Broadcast seed on cool spring soil for an early, scented display.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface and keep it damp through germination. Grow in lean, gritty soil and full sun for the strongest perfume. Small white flower heads carry a sweet scent through late spring; snip faded clusters to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring; this species self-sows readily in mild gardens.

directions: Sow seed thinly into loose, free-draining soil and cover only lightly. Once up, thin the seedlings and water sparingly, as this candytuft tolerates dry, poor ground. Pale lilac to white blooms appear in flattened heads by early summer; leave a few to ripen if you want volunteer seedlings next year.

planting.note: Direct sow this dwarf strain in early spring for a compact edging.

directions: Sow seed where it is to bloom and cover with a fine layer of soil, thinning once seedlings crowd. This compact mix blends white, rose, pink, lilac, and carmine domes on tidy mounds. Flowers begin under two months from sowing; light shearing after the first flush encourages fresh bloom.

planting.note: Sow in place in cool spring soil for a bright, quick-blooming patch.

directions: Scatter seed on prepared ground and rake in lightly, keeping the bed moist until germination. This strain shows strong, saturated shades of rose, crimson, purple, and white. Rounded flower clusters open quickly and draw bees and butterflies; deadhead to prolong the show.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; a strong choice for cutting rows.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin the seedlings to give each plant room to build a full spike. Grow in fertile, well-drained soil and stake in windy sites. Large pure-white flower heads elongate into hyacinth-like columns; harvest stems for arrangements when the lowest florets have opened.

planting.note: Set plants out only after all danger of frost has passed and nights stay warm.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and press it into moist mix without covering, since light aids germination. Keep the flat warm and humid until sprouts appear, then move to bright indirect light. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching, and expect continuous bloom from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Transplant into beds after the soil has warmed and frost risk is over.

directions: Start seed indoors eight to ten weeks before your frost-free date, leaving the seed uncovered on the surface. This compact, early-flowering series mounds low and spreads to knit into a solid carpet of color. Water evenly and feed lightly for bloom that carries through the shady season.

planting.note: Move seedlings to the garden once nights are reliably above the mid-fifties.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a warm, moist seed mix and keep it bright but out of direct sun. This large-flowered series covers a wide color range and blooms early on tidy plants. Deadheading is not needed, as spent flowers drop cleanly on their own.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into a shaded, evenly moist bed.

directions: Sow seed uncovered indoors and grow on in bright warmth until ready to plant. This series was bred for strong resistance to downy mildew, making it dependable where that disease has ended older impatiens plantings. Keep the soil consistently moist and it will bloom nonstop until the first hard freeze.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost in a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it warm and humid to germinate. Bred for high resistance to downy mildew, this series holds up in beds where standard busy Lizzie once failed. Water at the base to keep foliage dry and enjoy a full mound of bloom all summer.

planting.note: Transplant after frost once soil warms.

directions: Start seed on the surface of a warm, moist mix under bright light. This early, vigorous series fills in quickly for fast color in shaded beds and containers. Provide steady moisture and light feeding for bloom that lasts until frost.

planting.note: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in shade or dappled light.

directions: Grown from cuttings rather than seed, this series carries fully double, rose-like flowers on mounded plants. Plant into rich, well-drained soil or a container after frost and keep it evenly moist. The doubled blooms appear steadily through the warm months in shade to part sun.

planting.note: Set out cutting-grown plants after the last spring frost.

directions: This vegetatively propagated double impatiens produces ruffled, rosebud-style flowers on tidy, well-branched plants. Plant in shade to part shade with consistent moisture and a monthly feeding. Expect a dense flush of double blooms all season with no deadheading required.

planting.note: Plant into baskets after frost and let stems cascade over the rim.

directions: This trailing type spreads on lax stems that spill from hanging baskets and window boxes. Plant cutting-grown starts after frost and water often, since baskets dry quickly in warm weather. Trim back leggy runners at midseason to keep the plant full and flowering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun to part shade with room to grow large.

directions: This vigorous interspecific hybrid tolerates full sun and heat far better than ordinary impatiens, growing into a shrub-sized mound. Plant cutting-grown starts after frost and water generously, as the large plants are thirsty in hot weather. Feed regularly and give ample spacing for robust, season-long bloom.

planting.note: Set out after frost; unusually, this series grows true from seed.

directions: This is one of the few New Guinea impatiens series raised from seed rather than cuttings. Surface-sow in warmth under bright light, then transplant after frost into rich, evenly moist soil. Plants form compact mounds topped with large flowers from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in part sun.

directions: This vigorous cutting-grown series bears large flowers over dark green foliage on well-branched plants. Plant after frost in fertile soil with morning sun and afternoon shade, and keep the root zone evenly moist. It performs well in large containers and landscape beds through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; compact habit suits smaller pots.

directions: A dwarf version of the Celebration line, this series stays compact and tidy for small containers and edging. Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in part shade with steady moisture. Its neat mound stays covered in large flowers without pinching.

planting.note: Set out after frost in part sun with rich, moist soil.

directions: This cutting-grown series forms rounded, well-filled mounds that spread nicely in beds and containers. Plant after frost and keep evenly watered, giving morning sun with protection from harsh afternoon heat. Large flowers cover the plant from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost; early bloom makes it good for cool spring starts.

directions: This early-flowering cutting series blooms quickly and reliably across a broad color range. Plant after frost in fertile, moisture-retentive soil with part shade. Keep it evenly watered and it will hold large blooms above bronze-green foliage all season.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into part sun with consistent moisture.

directions: This uniform cutting-grown series offers a wide palette including selections with dark or variegated foliage. Plant after frost in rich soil and water steadily, favoring morning sun and afternoon shade. Its even, well-branched habit stays covered in large flowers into autumn.

planting.note: Plant after frost with generous spacing for its larger size.

directions: A large-growing cutting series, this impatiens produces oversized flowers on robust, spreading plants. Plant after frost in fertile soil, spacing generously so plants can reach full size. Water consistently and feed monthly for bold, season-long color in beds and big containers.

planting.note: Set out after frost; this series is grown from seed.

directions: This seed-raised New Guinea series forms compact, uniform plants suited to pots and mixed containers. Surface-sow in warmth under bright light, then transplant after frost into rich, moist soil. Give morning sun and afternoon shade for large flowers over dark foliage all summer.

planting.note: Plant into baskets after frost and let the stems cascade.

directions: This trailing interspecific impatiens spreads on spreading stems ideal for hanging baskets and container edges. Plant cutting-grown starts after frost and water frequently, since trailing plants dry out fast. It sun-tolerates better than common impatiens and blooms until frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost in sun or shade; tolerates both well.

directions: This interspecific hybrid resists downy mildew and thrives in both sun and shade, giving the look of classic impatiens with far more resilience. Plant cutting-grown starts after frost in moist, fertile soil. Its named trait is recovery, springing back to full bloom quickly after wilting during a dry spell.

planting.note: Plant after frost with room to spread; larger than standard Bounce.

directions: A larger-flowered, larger-plant version of the mildew-resistant Bounce line, thriving in sun to shade. Plant cutting-grown starts after frost with generous spacing in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Water steadily and it recovers rapidly from wilting to keep blooming until frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost in part sun to show off the bicolor blooms.

directions: This cutting-grown interspecific series is prized for its patterned, two-tone flowers with contrasting eyes and edges. Plant after frost in fertile, evenly moist soil with morning sun. Keep it watered and lightly fed for a steady show of bicolor blooms all season.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once the soil has warmed and frost danger has passed.

directions: Nick the hard seed coat with a file and soak seeds overnight, then sow half an inch deep beside a trellis or fence. Keep the soil evenly moist until vines climb, and avoid rich soil that pushes leaves at the expense of flowers. Funnel-shaped blooms open on sunny mornings from midsummer until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last spring frost when nights stay mild.

directions: Soak the seeds for several hours and sow about a half inch deep where vines can twine upward. Give them full sun and lean soil so the wide, sky-blue trumpets are not crowded out by foliage. Flowers unfurl each morning and fade by afternoon, repeating for months.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after frost, or start indoors a few weeks earlier in short-season areas.

directions: Scarify and soak the seeds, then plant half an inch deep at the base of a support. This species prizes large, richly colored blooms, so give it warmth, sun, and modest feeding. Expect showy morning flowers from summer into early autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Sow the small seeds a half inch deep beside a fence or netting and thin to a few strong vines. This species self-sows freely, so deadhead if you want to limit volunteers. Clusters of tiny scarlet trumpets draw hummingbirds from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors two to three weeks early.

directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then plant them a half inch deep by a support. This hybrid carries deeply lobed leaves and bright red star flowers that combine the best of its parents. Bloom runs from midsummer until frost and attracts hummingbirds daily.

planting.note: Sow directly once frost danger passes and soil is warm.

directions: Scarify and soak the seeds before sowing a half inch deep near a support. The three-lobed, ivy-like leaves and small sky-blue trumpets fold shut by midday. It self-seeds readily, so remove spent flowers to keep it in bounds.

planting.note: Sow after frost; this classic blooms later, so warm-start it in short seasons.

directions: Soak the seeds and sow a half inch deep against a tall trellis in full sun. Keep the soil on the lean side to encourage flowering rather than lush leaves. The huge, clear sky-blue trumpets with pale throats open each morning from late summer to frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in a warm, sunny position.

directions: Nick and soak seeds, then plant a half inch deep beside sturdy support. Give it sun and avoid over-feeding so the striking flowers keep coming. Large blooms are streaked and marbled in blue and white, no two exactly alike.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after frost when the soil is warm.

directions: Soak the seeds overnight and sow a half inch deep at the base of a trellis. Full sun and lean soil bring the best flowering on this vigorous vine. Pure white trumpets with soft cream-yellow throats open each morning through the season.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in warm soil.

directions: Scarify and soak seeds before sowing a half inch deep near support. Keep soil evenly moist while young and let the vine climb in full sun. Pale blue flowers carry a deeper blue star radiating from the throat.

planting.note: Sow after frost; a reliable self-sower once established.

directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then plant a half inch deep beside a trellis or fence. This treasured heirloom is easy and vigorous, self-seeding for years to come. Deep royal-purple flowers show a ruby-red star and magenta throat each morning.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and sow a half inch deep at the base of support. Full sun and lean soil keep this old cultivar flowering freely. The velvety flowers are the darkest purple-black in the group with a small pink throat.

planting.note: Sow after frost once the soil warms.

directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then sow a half inch deep by a trellis. Give it full sun and modest water for steady bloom into fall. Small deep-purple flowers glow with a red-purple star and pale center.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.

directions: Soak the seeds and plant a half inch deep at the foot of a support. Keep feeding light so the vine spends energy on blooms rather than foliage. Rich rosy-red flowers with a white throat open every morning through summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Scarify and soak seeds, then sow a half inch deep beside a trellis in full sun. Avoid overly rich soil to keep the vine flowering rather than running to leaf. White flowers are marked with a five-pointed reddish-purple star from the throat.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in full sun and warm soil.

directions: Soak the seeds overnight and sow a half inch deep at the base of a support. This award-winning selection blooms freely with light feeding and full sun. Large wine-red to rosy-crimson flowers open each morning from midsummer on.

planting.note: Sow after frost once soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then plant them a half inch deep by a trellis. Give full sun and even moisture for a long run of unusual flowers. The soft dusky rose-brown blooms have a warm cocoa cast that deepens toward the throat.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for earlier double flowers.

directions: Soak the seeds and sow a half inch deep against a support in full sun. This selection carries fully double flowers, so it does not set seed like single types. Ruffled powder-pink blooms look almost like small carnations along the vine.

planting.note: Sow after frost in warm, sunny soil.

directions: Scarify and soak seeds, then plant a half inch deep beside a trellis. Full sun and lean soil keep the semi-double blooms coming through summer. Frilly rosy-red flowers appear ruffled and layered, unusual among morning glories.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in full sun.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and sow a half inch deep at the base of a support. Give full sun and modest water for a steady show of patterned flowers. Blooms are splashed and streaked white over rosy-magenta, each one different.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after frost when soil warms.

directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then plant a half inch deep by a trellis in full sun. Keep the soil lean and let the vine climb freely for best flowering. Deep reddish-purple flowers carry a bold star pattern and open early in the day.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors in short seasons.

directions: Soak the seeds and sow a half inch deep near a trellis in full sun. This selection has softer, more restrained growth than many morning glories. Large picotee flowers show broad white edges and marbled centers of blue, red, or pink.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; blooms earlier than most, good for short seasons.

directions: Scarify and soak the seeds, then sow a half inch deep at the base of support. This early strain flowers weeks sooner than standard types, ideal for cooler regions. Large trumpets come in a mix of blue, rose, pink, and white shades.

planting.note: Sow after frost in warm soil and full sun.

directions: Soak seeds overnight and sow a half inch deep beside a trellis in full sun. Give it warmth and even moisture for a long run of variable flowers. Blooms swirl blue and white in shifting patterns so that no two look the same.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Nick and soak the seeds, then plant a half inch deep near support in full sun. This Japanese-type selection favors large, well-formed flowers over rampant growth. Blooms are blue with bold white picotee margins and pale marbled throats.

planting.note: Sow after frost once soil is warm.

directions: Soak the seeds and sow a half inch deep at the base of a trellis in full sun. Keep feeding light so the vine puts energy into its patterned flowers. Blooms are streaked and flaked white over blue and rosy-purple tones.

planting.note: Set plants out only after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Start seed indoors barely covered and keep the tray warm, as germination is slow and uneven over several weeks. Move seedlings out after frost into lean, well-drained soil and full sun, watering sparingly once roots take hold. Pinch young stems to build a bushy plant, and expect rounded clusters of tiny flowers from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring and leave the dead top growth on over winter for extra root protection in borderline zones.

directions: Plant this vigorous, unusually cold-hardy selection in spring in full sun and well-drained ground where it can reach shrub size. Avoid heavy fertilizing and overwatering, which reduce bloom. Warm orange, coral, and yellow flower heads cover the plant all summer and feed butterflies until frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost where its bright hot colors get plenty of sun.

directions: Plant this old, dependable variety in full sun and lean soil after the weather stays warm. It forms an upright, bushy plant that tolerates heat and drought with ease. Fiery orange-and-red flower clusters glow through the hottest months and draw butterflies.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a large, heat-loving color plant.

directions: Give this robust selection full sun and well-drained soil, planting after frost when the ground is warm. It grows into a large, mounding plant that shrugs off summer heat and needs little water once settled in. Clusters open yellow and orange, maturing to deep red for a multicolored display all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a proven hardy selection that returns reliably in the Southeast.

directions: Plant this low, wide-spreading and notably hardy variety in spring in full sun. It stays compact and blankets the ground with clear yellow flower clusters from late spring to frost. Leave winter growth in place in colder zones to help the crown survive.

planting.note: Plant in spring for a hardy, ground-covering habit and warm gold color.

directions: Set plants in full sun and well-drained soil in spring, spacing them to knit together as a low mat. Blooms open with a rosy blush and mature to golden yellow, covering the mound all summer. This selection overwinters reliably farther north than most lantanas.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun to enjoy the changing pastel color mix.

directions: Plant after frost in a hot, sunny spot with soil that drains freely. Flower heads blend pink, yellow, and cream, shifting shades as they age for a confetti effect. Trim lightly midsummer to keep the plant tidy and blooming heavily.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for bold two-tone bloom color.

directions: Give this compact variety full sun and lean, well-drained soil, planting once nights stay warm. Bright magenta and yellow flowers pack the rounded clusters and hold their color in heat. Water modestly and deadhead spent heads to encourage more bloom.

planting.note: Plant after frost where the gold-splashed foliage lights up a sunny bed.

directions: Plant this variegated selection, sometimes sold as Lemon Swirl, in full sun after frost. The green-and-gold marbled leaves are a feature in themselves, set off by clear yellow flower clusters. It grows best warm and dry, so avoid rich soil and heavy watering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for cool white and cream bloom.

directions: Set plants in a warm, sunny bed with well-drained soil after frost. Clusters open pale yellow and mature to clean white, giving a soft, cooling effect in summer heat. Keep it on the dry side and trim as needed to maintain the mound.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a neat, low golden mound.

directions: Plant this tidy, low-growing variety in full sun and freely draining soil once the weather warms. Flowers open orange and settle to bright gold, covering the compact mound all season. Its restrained size suits edging, mass plantings, and large containers.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun where it can spread as a low, colorful cover.

directions: Plant this wide-spreading selection after frost in a sunny, well-drained spot. It hugs the ground and fills space quickly with warm sunset-toned flower clusters of orange, red, and gold. Little care is needed beyond occasional watering in drought.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a colorful, compact garden plant.

directions: Set plants in full sun and lean soil after frost, spacing for good airflow. This member of the Patriot group stays compact and blooms in a bright multicolor blend of pink, orange, and yellow. Keep it dry rather than rich and it will flower without pause.

planting.note: Plant after frost where the trailing stems can spill over edges.

directions: Plant this white-flowered trailing form after frost in full sun and well-drained soil. Its low, cascading stems make it ideal for baskets, walls, and slopes. Small white clusters with tiny yellow eyes appear steadily through the warm months with little care.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for early, heavy bloom on a compact plant.

directions: Plant this compact Bandana series selection in full sun after frost and space it closely for a solid mound of color. It blooms early and stays neat, with clusters that shift from orange to fiery red. Deadheading is not required, though a light trim keeps it fresh.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a tidy, free-blooming mound.

directions: Give this compact variety full sun and well-drained soil, planting after frost. The flower clusters blend cherry-red, orange, and yellow on a small, uniform plant well suited to containers and edging. It flowers heavily from spring to frost and needs only occasional watering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for soft rose and yellow bloom.

directions: Plant after frost in a hot, sunny spot with soil that drains well. Clusters open with a rosy pink and yellow center, deepening as they mature on a compact, mounding plant. Keep it lean and dry for the heaviest flowering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a big, fast-filling color plant.

directions: Plant this vigorous Luscious series selection in full sun after frost and give it room to fill out. Flower heads mix orange, yellow, and red in a citrus blend that holds up through intense heat. It thrives in containers and landscapes with minimal watering once established.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for berry-toned flowers all summer.

directions: Set this robust variety in full sun and well-drained soil after frost. The clusters blend pink, purple, and cream in a soft berry palette that keeps coming through the hottest weather. Water sparingly and let it spread into a broad, mounding plant.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for unusual purple bloom.

directions: Plant after frost in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. This selection is prized for its rare, near-solid purple flower clusters that stand out among warmer-toned lantanas. It tolerates heat and drought and blooms steadily into fall.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for warm orange bloom on a compact plant.

directions: Give this compact selection full sun and well-drained soil, planting after frost. Rich marmalade-orange flower clusters cover the mounded plant from spring to frost. Keep it dry rather than pampered for the best flowering and butterfly traffic.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for early, uniform gold bloom.

directions: Plant this well-branched Landmark series variety in full sun after frost. It blooms early and evenly with clear golden-yellow clusters on a compact, mounded plant. Its uniform habit suits mass plantings, borders, and mixed containers with little upkeep.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a warm rose-and-yellow blend.

directions: Set plants in full sun and freely draining soil after frost. The clusters blend rose-pink, yellow, and cream for a sunrise effect on a tidy, mounded plant. It flowers dependably in heat and asks only for occasional water once established.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a small, tidy mound of hot color.

directions: Plant this dwarf Lucky series selection in full sun after frost, spacing closely for solid color. Its short, well-branched habit is ideal for small containers and front-of-border planting. Flame-red and orange clusters bloom nonstop through summer with little care.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for compact, cheerful yellow bloom.

directions: Give this dwarf variety full sun and well-drained soil, planting after frost. It stays small and neat, covered in clear yellow flower clusters from spring to frost. Its compact size makes it a reliable filler for pots and tight garden spaces.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for cool white bloom on a small plant.

directions: Plant this compact white selection in full sun after frost in well-drained soil. Clean white clusters with soft yellow centers give a cooling look and combine well with brighter lantanas. It blooms steadily in heat and needs only light, occasional watering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for soft peach tones on a dwarf plant.

directions: Plant this dwarf Little Lucky selection in full sun after frost, spacing tightly for a low carpet of bloom. The clusters glow in gentle peach, apricot, and yellow shades on a compact, well-branched plant. It performs well in small pots and edging with minimal care.

common_name: Little Lucky Pot of Gold Lantana
scientific_name: Lantana camara 'Little Lucky Pot of Gold'

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a small, gold-flowered mound.

directions: Set this dwarf variety in full sun and well-drained soil after frost. It forms a low, dense mound covered in bright golden-yellow clusters from spring to frost. Its small stature suits containers, edging, and the front of sunny beds.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun; a sterile type that avoids reseeding.

directions: Plant this sterile, compact selection in full sun after frost in well-drained soil. Because it sets little or no seed, it flowers heavily and does not spread by berries, making it a good choice where lantana can become weedy. Warm red and orange clusters bloom from spring to frost.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun; sterile and non-reseeding.

directions: Give this sterile, compact variety full sun and freely draining soil, planting after frost. It produces little viable seed, so it blooms continuously and will not spread invasively. Soft rose, pink, and yellow clusters cover the plant through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for a soft pink-and-yellow blend.

directions: Plant this selection in full sun and lean, well-drained soil after frost. Clusters open yellow and blush to soft pink for a gentle, warm pastel effect. It tolerates heat and drought and blooms steadily with only occasional watering.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for soft pink and cream bloom.

directions: Set plants in a sunny, well-drained spot after frost. The flower clusters blend pink, cream, and pale yellow for a soft, muted look uncommon among lantanas. Keep it dry and lean, trimming lightly as needed to maintain shape.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun for bright bicolor yellow-and-white bloom.

directions: Plant this compact variety in full sun after frost in well-drained soil. Its clusters show a cheerful bicolor of golden yellow centers ringed in white. It stays neat, blooms heavily, and needs little more than sun, warmth, and occasional water.

planting.note: An old bicolor best sown cool in early spring or overwintered in mild areas.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow an inch deep beside sturdy supports the plants can climb with their tendrils. This 1699 heirloom carries small maroon-and-violet bicolor flowers with an exceptionally strong scent. Cut stems in the cool of the morning and keep picking to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Deeply scented old form; sow cool in spring or autumn in frost-free winters.

directions: Chit the seed on damp paper before sowing an inch deep at the foot of a support. The rich purple-and-maroon blooms are among the most fragrant of all sweet peas. Harvest often and the vines will flower for many weeks.

planting.note: A very old pink-and-white bicolor from the 1700s; grow cool.

directions: Soak seed overnight and set it an inch deep where the vines can grab a trellis. Two-tone blooms show rose-pink upper petals over creamy white wings and carry a sweet perfume. Keep flowers cut to hold off seed set and lengthen the display.

planting.note: An 1898 grandiflora with deep maroon flowers; best in cool weather.

directions: Nick and soak the seed, then sow an inch deep near netting or canes. The dark chocolate-maroon blooms are richly scented and lovely against silver foliage. Cut stems frequently to keep the vine productive.

planting.note: An old striped grandiflora dating to the 1890s; grow cool.

directions: Presoak seed and sow one inch deep against a support for the tendrils. Flowers are boldly striped and flaked in crimson over a white ground, with a fine fragrance. Pick regularly and the plants will bloom for weeks.

planting.note: A classic crimson grandiflora from the early 1900s.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep at the base of a trellis. Bright crimson-scarlet blooms are strongly scented and hold well in the vase. Deadhead and cut often to keep flowers coming.

planting.note: An old pure-white grandiflora prized for scent.

directions: Chit or soak the seed and sow an inch deep beside supports. Large clean-white flowers are held on good stems with a lovely fragrance. Keep cutting through the season for a steady supply of blooms.

planting.note: A deep navy-blue grandiflora, also sold as Boreatton.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow an inch deep at the foot of netting. The deep indigo-blue blooms are heavily perfumed and stand out in any planting. Cut spikes as they open to prolong flowering.

planting.note: A rare orange-flowered grandiflora; shade blooms from strong sun to hold color.

directions: Soak and sow the seed an inch deep near a climbing frame. The glowing orange-salmon blooms are fragrant, though the color can fade in fierce sun. Pick often to keep new flowers forming.

planting.note: A bright blue grandiflora from the early 1900s.

directions: Presoak the seed and set it an inch deep against supports for the tendrils. Clear bright-blue flowers carry the classic sweet-pea perfume. Harvest stems in the morning and keep cutting to extend bloom.

planting.note: A soft rose-pink grandiflora from 1903.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep beside canes or netting. The gently shaded pink blooms are sweetly scented and good for cutting. Deadhead spent flowers so the vine keeps producing.

common_name: Captain of the Blues Sweet Pea

planting.note: An old purple-blue grandiflora from the 1880s.

directions: Nick and soak the seed, then sow an inch deep at a support. Blooms open mauve and mature to purple-blue with a rich scent. Cut regularly to keep the plants in flower.

planting.note: An old orange-pink grandiflora; shade from hottest sun.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow an inch deep near a climbing frame. Warm salmon-orange flowers glow against fresh green foliage and smell sweet. Keep picking to prolong the flowering season.

planting.note: The original frilled Spencer type from 1901; grow cool.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow an inch deep at the base of tall supports. This first Spencer variety bears large ruffled pink blooms with wavy petals. Cut long stems often and the vine keeps flowering.

planting.note: A pure-white large-flowered Spencer, popular for bouquets.

directions: Soak seed overnight and sow an inch deep beside a trellis. Big frilled white flowers on long stems are ideal for the cutting garden and lightly scented. Harvest as blooms open to keep new spikes forming.

planting.note: A very dark maroon Spencer; the deepest tones show in cool weather.

directions: Nick and soak the seed, then sow an inch deep against supports. Deep near-black maroon flowers have a velvety sheen and pleasant scent. Cut often to keep the color and flowers coming.

planting.note: A wine-red Spencer named for the color of the grape.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep at the foot of a support. Rich burgundy-claret blooms on long stems make dramatic cut flowers and carry a good scent. Keep picking so the vine stays productive.

planting.note: A pink picotee Spencer; an award-winning exhibition type.

directions: Presoak seed and sow an inch deep beside canes or netting. Large frilled blooms are white flushed with rose-pink at the petal edges. Cut long stems frequently to hold the plant in flower.

planting.note: A cream picotee Spencer edged in rose-pink.

directions: Soak and sow the seed an inch deep at a support. Cream flowers rimmed with soft pink are beautifully fragrant and carried on strong stems. Harvest often to keep new blooms coming.

planting.note: A chocolate-maroon flaked and rippled Spencer on white.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow an inch deep at the base of netting. Petals are marbled and streaked in maroon over a pale ground for a striking look. Cut spikes as they open to prolong the display.

planting.note: A blue-flaked rippled Spencer on a white ground.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep beside a trellis. White petals are edged and streaked with clear blue for a fresh two-tone effect. Keep cutting to lengthen the flowering season.

planting.note: A smoky slate-streaked Spencer with unusual coloring.

directions: Presoak seed and sow an inch deep at supports. Petals are feathered in dusky purple-grey over a cream base, giving a moody, marbled look. Cut stems often to keep flowers forming.

planting.note: A highly fragrant cream Spencer edged in lilac.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep near a climbing support. Ivory blooms picoteed with lilac are among the most powerfully scented of any variety. Cut the long stems often for the vase and to encourage more flowers.

planting.note: Also sold as April in Paris; renowned for its perfume.

directions: Presoak seed and sow an inch deep at the foot of netting. Cream flowers rimmed in soft blue-lilac fill the garden with strong fragrance. Keep picking to prolong the bloom.

planting.note: A white Spencer with a clean pink picotee edge.

directions: Soak and sow the seed an inch deep beside supports. Large ruffled white flowers are neatly edged in rose-pink and good for cutting. Harvest stems frequently to keep the plants blooming.

planting.note: A large lavender-blue Spencer with excellent scent.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow an inch deep at a trellis. Big frilled blooms in soft lavender-blue are carried on long stems, perfect for arrangements. Cut often to keep new flowers coming.

planting.note: A classic mid-blue Spencer and a proven show flower.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep beside canes. Large frilled blooms in bright blue-mauve are richly scented and hold well cut. Keep picking to extend flowering.

planting.note: A lavender Spencer favored by exhibitors.

directions: Presoak seed and sow an inch deep at a support. Soft lavender blooms with waved petals are lightly scented and good for cutting. Harvest often to keep the vine flowering.

planting.note: A bright scarlet-cerise Spencer; shade from harsh sun to hold color.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep beside supports. Vivid scarlet blooms make a bold splash in the garden and the vase. Cut stems regularly and the plant will keep flowering.

planting.note: A cream-white Spencer with large ruffled blooms.

directions: Presoak seed and sow an inch deep at a climbing frame. Soft ivory-cream flowers are borne on long stems ideal for cutting. Keep harvesting to prolong bloom.

planting.note: A reverse bicolor Spencer with an unusual color layout.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep at netting. The blooms carry lavender-blue wings under soft pink standards, reversing the usual pattern. Cut often to keep flowers coming.

common_name: Prince Edward of York Sweet Pea

planting.note: An old cerise-and-rose bicolor grandiflora from the 1890s.

directions: Presoak seed and sow an inch deep at a support. Bright cerise standards over rose-pink wings make a cheerful bicolor with strong scent. Harvest stems often to keep blooming.

planting.note: An old flaked grandiflora in chocolate over cream.

directions: Soak and sow the seed an inch deep beside supports. Petals are striped and marbled in chocolate-maroon over a creamy base. Cut regularly so the vine keeps producing.

planting.note: A blend of fragrant old grandiflora types in mixed colors; more heat-tolerant.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow an inch deep at a trellis. This heritage mix gives a range of colors on strongly scented smaller blooms that tolerate warmth better than modern types. Keep picking to prolong the display.

planting.note: A dwarf trailing type for edging, baskets, and containers.

directions: Soak the seed and sow half an inch to an inch deep in a pot or bed edge. This low, spreading form needs no support and trails nicely over container rims. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the mounds blooming.

planting.note: A compact bush type needing little or no staking.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow about an inch deep in beds or large pots. This tidy bush form makes a mound of scented mixed-color blooms without a trellis. Pinch young tips and deadhead to keep it flowering.

planting.note: A semi-dwarf bush type for containers and low borders.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep in the bed or a roomy container. Short, self-supporting plants carry a mix of colors on stems long enough to cut. Keep deadheading for a longer show.

planting.note: A knee-high bush type that benefits from light brushwood support.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow an inch deep in the bed, giving the plants a low twiggy support to lean on. This mid-height bush form yields plenty of fragrant mixed-color stems for cutting. Harvest often to keep the flowers coming.

planting.note: A compact, well-branched series bred for pots and baskets.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep in containers or bed edges. The bushy, self-supporting plants flower freely in a range of colors. Deadhead regularly to keep the display going.

planting.note: A dwarf bush series that needs no staking.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow an inch deep in beds or large pots. These self-supporting plants produce a spread of bright colors on decent stems. Keep deadheading and cutting to prolong bloom.

planting.note: An early, day-length-tolerant series for autumn sowing and early bloom under cover.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep at a support, autumn-sowing in mild areas for the earliest flowers. This early-blooming series comes into bloom weeks ahead of standard types. Cut long stems often to prolong the season.

planting.note: A blend of large frilled Spencer types in many colors.

directions: Presoak the seed and sow an inch deep at a tall support. This mixture gives big ruffled blooms in a wide color range on long cutting stems. Keep picking so the vines stay in flower for months.

planting.note: A vigorous, more heat-tolerant series in mixed colors.

directions: Soak the seed and sow an inch deep beside a trellis. This robust series holds up to heat better than many, giving fragrant blooms in a full color range. Harvest stems often to keep flowers forming.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost or start a few weeks early in pots to set out undisturbed.

directions: Press seed lightly under a quarter inch of soil in a sunny, well-drained bed. Water steadily while young, then let established plants dry slightly between soakings to keep them sturdy. Rich rose-pink cups veined a darker shade open all summer; deadhead spent flowers to prolong the show.

planting.note: Sow in place after frost; this compact form suits the front of a border.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and thin to about a foot apart once seedlings have several leaves. This bushy dwarf needs little support and blooms freely in full sun with even moisture. Look for pure white satiny flowers against dark green foliage from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil or set out pot-grown starts without disturbing the roots.

directions: Cover seed with a quarter inch of soil and keep the bed moist until sprouts appear. Give plants full sun and space to breathe, watering moderately and avoiding heavy feeding that softens growth. Deep cerise-pink blooms with darker veining crowd the stems through summer.

planting.note: Sow where plants will stand; this taller strain earns a spot at the back of a bed.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in a sunny, sheltered spot and thin generously to allow air movement. Stake or grow among sturdier neighbors, since the tall stems can lean in wind. Warm rose-pink trumpets appear in high summer and make good cut flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost for the least root disturbance.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the soil evenly moist while germinating. Site in full sun with free-draining soil and water moderately once established. Soft blush-pink flowers etched with fine deep-pink veins open across summer.

planting.note: Sow in place or in pots; this early dwarf even flowers well in containers.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and thin to a compact spacing suited to pots or edging. Because it blooms young and short, no staking is needed in most sites. Rose-pink flowers begin earlier than standard types, giving color from early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost for tidy, low mounds.

directions: Set seed a quarter inch deep and keep moist until seedlings emerge. This bushy dwarf holds itself without support and suits borders or large containers. Pale pink blooms flushed deeper toward the center cover the plant through summer.

planting.note: Sow where plants are to bloom; this vigorous strain fills space quickly.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in full sun and thin to give each plant elbow room. Water moderately and skip rich feeding to keep stems strong and self-supporting. Deep rose-pink flowers glow against the foliage from midsummer to first frost.

planting.note: Direct sow the blend after frost for a range of colors in one drift.

directions: Scatter and lightly cover seed in a prepared sunny bed, then thin to about a foot apart. This mixture yields white, blush, and deep-pink plants of even height for a tapestry effect. Blooms open across summer and last well when cut for the vase.

planting.note: Sow in place or in pots; this compact type performs well in containers.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm, well-drained soil and thin to a compact spacing. The dense, branching habit needs no staking and flowers heavily in full sun. Vivid deep-pink blooms cover the low mounds from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Begin indoors under lights and transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a warm, moist mix and cover very lightly, expecting germination over two to three weeks. This cut-flower strain gives long, sturdy stems in a broad blend of blue, rose, white, apricot, and yellow shades. Harvest when most florets on a spray have opened, and hang bunches upside down in a dry, airy spot to preserve them.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and move seedlings outdoors after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it evenly warm until it germinates in two to three weeks. This selection produces deep, saturated blue calyces on tall stems prized for bouquets. Cut sprays when nearly every floret has opened for the richest color, whether used fresh or dried.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out when nights stay above freezing.

directions: Barely cover the seed and hold it warm and moist for two to three weeks until it sprouts. This variety offers soft light-blue papery blooms that dry beautifully. Snip the stems once the spray is mostly open and bundle them to air-dry for lasting arrangements.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant after the frost-free date.

directions: Set seed on the surface of warm, damp mix and cover only lightly, watching for sprouts in two to three weeks. Rose-pink calyces top strong stems that stand up well in the vase. Cut when the flower spray is fully colored and open for the best fresh or dried display.

planting.note: Grow transplants indoors and set them out once frost has passed.

directions: Sow shallowly on a warm surface and keep just moist for two to three weeks until germination. Clean white papery blooms make this a versatile filler and a bright accent in dried work. Harvest sprays when they are fully open and hang them to dry in a shaded, breezy place.

planting.note: Begin indoors early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and lightly moist until it emerges in two to three weeks. Bright golden-yellow calyces bring warm color to fresh and dried bouquets alike. Cut the stems when the spray is fully open for the strongest, most lasting color.

planting.note: Start indoors and move seedlings out after frost danger ends.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix, cover lightly, and expect sprouts in two to three weeks. Deep purple papery blooms hold their color exceptionally well when dried. Cut sprays when most florets have opened and hang them upside down to preserve the tone.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out once nights warm.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep warm and damp for two to three weeks until it germinates. Soft lavender calyces suit both pastel bouquets and everlasting arrangements. Harvest when the spray is fully colored and open, then dry in a well-ventilated shady spot.

planting.note: Start indoors early for transplanting after the last frost.

directions: Sow shallowly on a warm, moist surface and watch for seedlings over two to three weeks. Warm apricot-peach calyces are an unusual and popular color for cutting. Snip the stems once the spray has mostly opened for the richest hue in fresh or dried use.

planting.note: Begin under lights indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Barely cover seed on a warm surface and keep it lightly moist until it sprouts in two to three weeks. Vivid carmine-red calyces add bold, deep color to mixed bunches. Cut when the flower spray is fully open, and hang to dry for long-lasting arrangements.

planting.note: Grow transplants indoors and plant out after the frost-free date.

directions: Surface-sow the seed, cover lightly, and hold it warm for two to three weeks until germination. Soft creamy champagne tones make an elegant neutral for wedding and dried work. Harvest sprays fully open and dry them upside down in a shaded, airy place.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep warm and moist for two to three weeks until sprouting. This vigorous series is bred for tall, well-branched stems and heavy yields in a wide color range. Cut sprays when nearly all florets have opened, and dry or use fresh as a long-lasting cut flower.

planting.note: Begin indoors and set plants out after frost danger ends.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and lightly damp until it emerges in two to three weeks. This blend leans toward warm pastel tones of apricot, peach, rose, and cream. Harvest the sprays fully open for the best color, then air-dry them for everlasting bouquets.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out once nights warm.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix and keep just moist for two to three weeks until germination. This series offers reliable, uniform plants across a full spectrum of statice colors. Cut the stems when the flower spray is mostly open and dry or arrange them as desired.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on a warm surface and expect sprouts in two to three weeks. This commercial cut-flower series is selected for long, strong stems and consistent color. Harvest sprays when the florets are fully open, and hang bunches to dry for lasting bouquets.

planting.note: Grow transplants indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and moist until it germinates over two to three weeks. This early, fast-finishing series flowers quickly on well-branched plants. Cut the sprays once most florets have opened for fresh use or drying.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after the frost-free date.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep warm and lightly moist for two to three weeks until it sprouts. This compact, well-branched dwarf mix suits edging, containers, and small bouquets. Cut or enjoy the short sprays once they are fully open and colored.

planting.note: Begin indoors early and transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow the seed, cover it lightly, and keep warm until germination in two to three weeks. This classic selection bears deep rose-pink calyces on tall, sturdy stems. Harvest when the spray is fully open and hang to dry for rich, lasting color.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed on a warm surface and keep it moist for two to three weeks until sprouting. This variety produces clear golden-yellow calyces that dry to a bright, lasting color. Cut the stems once the flower spray is fully open for fresh or everlasting use.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Sow shallowly on warm, damp mix and expect sprouts in two to three weeks. Pure white papery blooms make this a clean, versatile cut and dried flower. Harvest sprays fully open and dry them in a shaded, well-ventilated spot.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and hold it warm and moist for two to three weeks until it germinates. Intensely deep blue calyces give strong contrast in mixed bouquets and dried work. Cut the sprays when nearly every floret has opened to lock in the darkest color.

planting.note: Begin indoors early and plant out after the frost-free date.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and lightly damp until sprouts appear in two to three weeks. Soft light-rose calyces suit gentle pastel arrangements. Harvest when the flower spray is fully open, then dry the bunches upside down for lasting color.

planting.note: Scatter seed in early spring while nights are still cool, as bloom fades in summer heat.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto firmed soil and barely cover it, keeping the surface moist until sprouts appear. Thin seedlings to a few inches apart and pinch young tips once to encourage branching. Expect clouds of small snapdragon-like blooms in about eight to ten weeks; shear spent plants to coax a second flush.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool spring soil; this dwarf mix flowers fastest before summer heat.

directions: Sprinkle seed over raked soil and press in without burying, since light aids sprouting. Keep evenly damp and thin to a hand's width once true leaves form. This compact strain opens a pastel mix of rose, yellow, lavender and white spikes roughly two months from sowing.

planting.note: Sow directly in early spring for tall, bright spikes suited to cutting.

directions: Broadcast the fine seed onto smooth soil and firm it in lightly rather than covering it. Water gently and thin crowded seedlings so air moves freely between them. This taller mix blooms in a wide spread of jewel tones and makes a fine small-scale cut flower once the lower florets open.

planting.note: A dwarf bedding mix best direct sown while spring soil is cool.

directions: Scatter seed thinly over prepared ground and press it against the surface without a soil cover. Mist to keep moist through germination, then thin to space plants a few inches apart. This low, bushy mix carries multicolored miniature blooms that work well edging beds and filling containers.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool spring soil for its deep crimson-and-gold spikes.

directions: Press seed lightly into a fine seedbed and leave it uncovered so light can reach it. Keep the surface damp until seedlings emerge, then thin to a few inches. Prized for its rich carmine flowers marked with an orange throat, it blooms about ten weeks from sowing and rewards deadheading.

planting.note: Sow in place in spring; this species runs taller than the Moroccan sorts.

directions: Sow seed on the soil surface and firm it gently, keeping it moist through the germination window. Thin the upright seedlings and stake taller stands in windy sites. Expect deep purple, gold-flushed flowers on branching stems from early summer, useful both in the border and as a cut stem.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring for tall, fiery-toned flowering stems.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the seedbed damp until sprouts stand up. Thin plants and support the tallest stems if the site is exposed. This selection is grown for its hot blend of orange, red and gold blooms carried well above the foliage for cutting.

planting.note: Cool-season annual best sown directly in early spring.

directions: Scatter seed over firmed soil, press it in, and leave uncovered for light. Keep damp until germination, then thin the slender seedlings. This dainty species bears violet-purple flowers with a split lower lip and self-sows freely where happy.

planting.note: Sow or set out in spring; the soft pink form comes largely true from seed.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface and keep it lightly moist until it sprouts. Grow in lean, sharply drained ground and give each plant room to form a slender clump. Its wands of pale pink flowers bloom for months and draw bees; allow some seeding to keep the color coming.

planting.note: Spring sow this white-flowered form; propagate by division to keep it pure.

directions: Surface sow and firm the seed in without covering, keeping moist through germination. Plant in poor, free-draining soil and full sun for the sturdiest spikes. Airy stems of pure white blossoms appear through summer; divide clumps in spring to preserve the true white strain.

planting.note: Sow in spring; note it spreads by roots and can become weedy or invasive.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on the surface and keep the ground moist until seedlings appear, which can be slow and uneven. Site it where its running roots can be contained, as it spreads readily. Yellow snapdragon-like flowers with an orange throat bloom from midsummer to frost and are a favorite of long-tongued bees.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; this Iberian species is only borderline hardy.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a gritty mix and keep at room temperature until seedlings appear. Grow on and plant out after frost in a sheltered, part-shaded spot with good drainage. The purple-pink flowers cluster in threes that resemble little birds on a wire, opening through summer into autumn.

planting.note: Grown for its bold yellow spikes, but a listed noxious weed in much of the western US.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep moist through a sometimes slow germination. Give it lean, dry, well-drained soil in full sun where its aggressive roots will not escape into wild land. Waxy blue-green foliage carries tall spires of bright yellow, orange-throated flowers all summer; check local regulations before planting.

planting.note: Sow in winter and give a cold period; a cushion plant for troughs and rock gardens.

directions: Sow onto a gritty surface and expose the pot to winter cold to break dormancy. Grow the seedlings hard in very sharp drainage, as it resents wet and rich soil. This tiny alpine forms a low mat of blue-grey leaves topped by violet flowers with an orange spot, blooming through the cool months of the growing season.

planting.note: Move hardened seedlings to the garden around the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors and leave it uncovered under bright light, keeping the mix consistently moist until sprouts appear in two to three weeks. This compact classic pairs deep violet-blue flowers with distinctive bronze-tinged foliage, so give it full sun to hold the dark leaf color. Plant along bed edges after frost and enjoy dense bloom through the cooler months of the season.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost when the soil has warmed slightly.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a damp seed-starting mix and mist gently, holding warmth and light until the seedlings emerge. This tidy mounding form carries soft powder-blue flowers over green foliage and looks best massed as an edging. Keep the roots evenly moist through summer and trim after the first flush to encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant out once frost has passed and evenings are no longer cold.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the surface of moist starting mix and do not bury it, since light aids germination over the next couple of weeks. This compact selection blooms pure white and gives a clean, cooling edge to summer borders and containers. Give it steady moisture and shear it back lightly in midsummer to keep the mounds fresh and full.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost date once nights stay above the mid-forties.

directions: Sow indoors on the surface of a fine, damp medium and keep it bright and warm until the seedlings develop. This compact form is prized for its rich carmine-red flowers, each brightened by a small white eye. Plant in full sun with reliable moisture and pinch spent growth to prolong the display into autumn.

planting.note: Transplant hardened seedlings around the last spring frost.

directions: Start the fine seed indoors uncovered on moist mix, giving it light and gentle bottom warmth to germinate. This long-grown favorite bears deep blue flowers each set off by a crisp white central eye, forming neat compact mounds. Keep it watered through heat and clip lightly after the first bloom wave to keep flowers coming.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost when the ground has begun to warm.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a damp tray and keep it lit and moist until sprouts show in a few weeks. This heirloom edging type forms low, even cushions covered in clear light-blue flowers. Plant it as a border ribbon in full sun, water steadily, and shear midseason to renew the mounds.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost once cold nights have ended.

directions: Surface-sow the dust-fine seed on moist mix and leave it uncovered under bright conditions for two to three weeks. This blend produces compact mounds in a mix of blue, lilac, rose, and white flowers for a lively multicolor edge. Give even moisture and full sun, and cut back after the first flush to keep the color coming.

planting.note: Set into baskets or beds after the last frost has passed.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface of a damp medium, keeping it warm and lit until the seedlings appear. This cascading type spills deep blue flowers, each marked with a white eye, over the sides of baskets and walls. Water generously so the trailing stems never dry out, and shear back in midsummer to spark a fresh flush.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost since this double form is grown vegetatively.

directions: Because its flowers are fully double and set little viable seed, this old trailing selection is raised from cuttings and sold as plants. Set transplants at the same depth as their pots in rich, moisture-retentive soil or basket mix. Water often and trim leggy runners to keep the double sky-blue blossoms crowding the plant all season.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost when nights turn mild.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface indoors and keep it uncovered, warm, and moist until it sprouts in a couple of weeks. This early, uniform member of the compact Riviera series carries white-and-blue bicolor flowers for a bright edging effect. Plant in full sun with even watering and shear lightly in midsummer to refresh the bloom.

planting.note: Set out around the last frost once cold snaps have ended.

directions: Press seed onto damp starting mix without covering, holding steady warmth and light through germination. This compact Riviera color blooms in a rich mid-blue with a small pale eye and stays neat and early. Give it consistent moisture and full sun, then trim after the first flush to keep the mounds tidy and floriferous.

planting.note: Transplant after frost passes and the soil begins to warm.

directions: Surface-sow indoors on a moist medium and keep the tray bright until seedlings emerge over two to three weeks. This compact Riviera selection opens soft sky-blue flowers early and evenly across low cushions. Plant as a border edge in full sun, water regularly, and shear midseason for a lasting show.

planting.note: Move outdoors once frost danger is over.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface of damp mix, leaving it uncovered under good light to germinate. This compact Riviera color trades the usual blue for warm rose-pink flowers, offering a softer edging option. Keep evenly moist in full sun and cut back after the first bloom to renew the mound.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost with mild nights ahead.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on moist starting mix and press without covering, keeping it warm and bright to sprout. This compact Riviera form blooms clean white and blends well with bolder companions in beds and pots. Provide steady water and full sun, then shear lightly to keep it flowering through the cooler stretches of summer.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings around the last spring frost.

directions: Sow on the surface of a damp fine mix indoors and hold warmth and light for two to three weeks until germination. This compact Riviera color offers gentle lilac-purple flowers over even green cushions. Plant in full sun with regular moisture and trim after the first flush to keep it looking fresh.

planting.note: Set out once frost has passed and the soil warms.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix and leave uncovered under bright light until seedlings appear. This compact Riviera selection shows off the deepest, most saturated blue of the series over tidy mounds. Give it full sun and even water, then shear midseason to draw out a second wave of flowers.

planting.note: Plant into baskets or beds after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep the seed uncovered, warm, and moist until it germinates in two to three weeks. This trailing Regatta color pours out white-and-blue bicolor flowers and tolerates heat better than older types. Grow in baskets or as a spiller with generous water, and shear back midsummer to prompt fresh trailing growth.

planting.note: Transplant after frost once nights are consistently mild.

directions: Start the fine seed on the surface of damp mix indoors, providing light and warmth for germination. This trailing Regatta selection carries deep marine-blue flowers and holds up longer into summer heat. Keep basket or container mix constantly moist and clip runners in midseason to keep the cascade full.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost date.

directions: Sow indoors on the surface of a moist medium and leave uncovered until seedlings develop under bright light. This trailing Regatta color spills soft sky-blue flowers and is bred for stronger summer performance. Water baskets often so stems never wilt, and shear midsummer to trigger renewed bloom.

planting.note: Transplant after frost passes into warm soil.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto damp starting mix indoors and keep it lit and moist to germinate. This trailing Regatta selection offers warm rose-pink flowers instead of the familiar blues, softening mixed baskets. Provide plenty of water and part sun, and trim in midsummer to keep the cascade blooming.

planting.note: Plant out once the last frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors and hold warmth and light until it sprouts over a couple of weeks. This trailing Regatta color blooms crisp white and brightens the edges of mixed containers and baskets. Keep it well watered in part sun and shear back midseason to renew the trailing stems.

planting.note: Set out after frost when evenings warm up.

directions: Start seed on the surface of a damp mix indoors and leave uncovered under bright light to germinate. This trailing Regatta selection carries soft lilac-purple flowers that spill nicely from hanging baskets. Keep the mix evenly moist and trim runners in midsummer to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warmed soil.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist starting mix and keep it uncovered, warm, and lit until seedlings appear. This trailing Regatta color delivers the deepest dark blue of the series over cascading stems. Grow with steady water in part sun and shear midsummer to spur a fresh flush of bloom.

planting.note: Plant into baskets and beds after frost danger ends.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed indoors and keep it uncovered and evenly moist under light until it germinates. This trailing mix blooms in a tumble of blue, violet, lilac, rose, and white flowers for a colorful cascade. Water baskets generously and shear back midseason to keep the many-colored curtain full into fall.
